nativeRange.habitat: Coral-rich lagoons and outer reef slopes between approximately 3 and 60 m, often in areas of high reef relief; juveniles tend to favour more sheltered inner reefs.

diet.notes: In the wild has been documented feeding on sponges, tunicates and macroalgae. In aquaria a varied diet is generally advised, including Spirulina, marine algae sheets, high-quality angelfish preparations with sponge content, mysis and finely chopped frozen seafood. Multiple small feedings per day tend to be better tolerated than one large meal.

summary: A large Indo-Pacific marine angelfish with bold six-barred adult patterning; generally considered semi-aggressive, reef-cautious, and best suited to experienced keepers with very large systems.
careNotes: The Six Bar Angelfish (Pomacanthus sexstriatus) is one of the larger Pomacanthus angelfishes, reaching around 40-46 cm as an adult. It is generally advised to provide a minimum tank of around 220 US gallons (~833 L) with extensive live rock, deep caves for refuge and substantial open swimming space across a long footprint. Juveniles and adults display strikingly different colour patterns: juveniles are dark blue-black with curving white vertical bands, while adults show the characteristic dark vertical bars on a pale body with blue polka-dot fins, and the colour transition can take many months in captivity. It is generally considered reef-safe with caution as it has been reported to nip stony and soft corals as well as clam mantles. Keep only one per system and avoid combining with other large angelfishes of similar size. Like other large Pomacanthus species, it is reportedly prone to Head and Lateral Line Erosion when kept in poor water quality or on a vitamin-poor diet, so stable parameters and regular feeding of marine-algae-based foods are commonly considered important. Adults are known to produce loud grunting sounds when disturbed. Cross-source notes: LiveAquaria lists 22-26 degC and FishBase wild range 25.5-29 degC; a middle range of 23-27 degC is used here. LiveAquaria does not state a captive lifespan; the 15-20 year figure reflects typical large-Pomacanthus longevity reports and should be treated as approximate.
breedingNotes: Not known to be bred in home aquaria. Like other large Pomacanthus, the species is widely reported to be a protogynous (female-first) sequential hermaphrodite.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Cross-checked adult size (46 cm TL vs LiveAquaria 18"/45.7 cm — agreement), wild temperature range (25.5-29 degC mean 28.1 vs LiveAquaria captive 22-26 degC — middle range used), native distribution, family/order, and habitat depth range.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Sixbar angelfish)
additional.notes: Tertiary cross-check: confirmed max size ~46 cm, distribution from Sri Lanka to Solomon Islands and Ryukyus to Australia, family Pomacanthidae / order Acanthuriformes, omnivorous diet on macroalgae/sponges/tunicates, distinct juvenile colour phase, and grunting-when-disturbed behaviour.

fish.breedingNotes: Protogynous hermaphrodite in the wild; captive breeding is essentially undocumented.
